prompt: A witty cartoon-like educational illustration with clean lines and a warm color palette: a friendly cartoon character feeds a tangled wiggly ribbon of raw squiggle into one side of a small smooth machine, and out the other side floats a tidy little constellation of glowing dots arranged neatly in open space, with a few similar dots clustering close together and dissimilar ones drifting comfortably apart, the messy ribbon becoming a clean map of points. alt: A cartoon character feeds a tangled wiggly ribbon into a small machine that emits a tidy constellation of glowing points where similar ones cluster and different ones spread apart, picturing how a learned representation turns a raw messy time series into a compact geometry of coordinates.
caption: Learning a representation means trading a tangled raw signal for a tidy map of coordinates, where distance finally starts to mean similarity.

after_heading: 1. The Self-Supervised Idea: Inventing a Label From the Data
prompt: A witty cartoon-like educational illustration with clean lines and a warm color palette: a resourceful cartoon character covers part of a wavy strip with its own hand, then quizzes itself by pointing at the hidden gap and guessing what shape lies underneath, grading its own guess against the strip it secretly already holds, a one-character self-taught pop quiz with no teacher in sight, the answer key being the very data it covered. Style friendly and approachable like a clever XKCD or Kurzgesagt panel, expressive face and body language, one clear visual gag of a model quizzing itself with a hidden answer it already owns, minimal clutter, generous negative space, suitable for a technical textbook. No text, letters, numbers, or labels anywhere in the image.
alt: A cartoon character hides part of a wavy strip under its own hand, quizzes itself on the hidden gap, and grades the guess against the strip it already holds, dramatizing how self-supervised learning invents a free training label by hiding part of the data and predicting it.
caption: Self-supervision is a pop quiz a model sets for itself: it hides part of the data, predicts the missing piece, and grades against the answer it already had.

prompt: A witty cartoon-like educational illustration with clean lines and a warm color palette: two cartoon characters each gently reshape a copy of the same heartbeat-like waveform, one calmly adds a faint shimmer and a slight stretch while keeping the telltale spikes intact, the other overzealously flips and scrambles its copy until the original shape is unrecognizable and it stares back baffled at the mangled result, contrasting a respectful tweak against an augmentation that destroyed the very meaning it was meant to keep. Style friendly and approachable like a clever XKCD or Kurzgesagt panel, expressive faces and body language, one clear visual gag of a too-aggressive transform erasing the signal it should preserve, minimal clutter, generous negative space, suitable for a technical textbook. No text, letters, numbers, or labels anywhere in the image.
alt: Two cartoon characters reshape copies of the same heartbeat waveform, one gently adds a shimmer while keeping the key spikes and the other flips and scrambles it into an unrecognizable mess, dramatizing how an over-aggressive augmentation can destroy the very semantics it was supposed to preserve.
caption: A good augmentation roughs up the surface while keeping the meaning intact; push too hard and you teach the model that two totally different things are the same.

after_heading: 1. The Contrastive Principle and the InfoNCE Loss
prompt: A witty cartoon-like educational illustration with clean lines and a warm color palette: a cartoon character stands among floating dots and tugs one nearly-identical twin dot snugly toward an anchor dot with a short taut elastic band, while at the same time firmly nudging a crowd of unrelated stranger dots away to the edges of the open space, the scene a clear push and pull where the matched pair magnetically attracts and the mismatched crowd politely repels. Style friendly and approachable like a clever XKCD or Kurzgesagt panel, expressive body language, one clear visual idea of pulling positives together and pushing negatives apart in an embedding space, minimal clutter, generous negative space, suitable for a technical textbook. No text, letters, numbers, or labels anywhere in the image.
alt: A cartoon character pulls a near-identical twin dot toward an anchor with a taut elastic band while pushing a crowd of unrelated stranger dots away to the edges, picturing how a contrastive loss draws matched positive pairs together and repels mismatched negatives in the embedding space.
caption: Contrastive learning is a game of magnetic manners: pull the matched pair close, push every stranger away, and a meaningful geometry falls out for free.

prompt: A witty cartoon-like educational illustration with clean lines and a warm color palette: a smug cartoon character has found a lazy shortcut, it has squashed every single dot in the whole space into one tiny identical blob sitting in its palm, every input collapsed to the exact same point, the character grinning at having technically made everything match while a tiny dismayed onlooker realizes the map now says nothing because every place is the same place. Style friendly and approachable like a clever XKCD or Kurzgesagt panel, expressive faces and body language, one clear visual gag of representational collapse where all points become one, minimal clutter, generous negative space, suitable for a technical textbook. No text, letters, numbers, or labels anywhere in the image.
alt: A smug cartoon character squashes every dot in the space into one identical blob in its palm so that all inputs map to the same point while a dismayed onlooker realizes the map now distinguishes nothing, dramatizing representational collapse where an encoder cheats by sending everything to one constant.
caption: Collapse is the trivial cheat that haunts non-contrastive methods: map every input to one point and the loss is happy, even though the representation now knows nothing.

after_heading: 3. Masked Versus Contrastive: When Each Wins, and Combining Them
prompt: A witty cartoon-like educational illustration with clean lines and a warm color palette: two contrasting cartoon workers each rebuild understanding of the same wavy strip in their own way, one patiently fills in small blanked-out gaps of the strip patch by patch to reconstruct fine local detail, the other steps back and sorts whole strips into who-matches-whom piles to capture the big global gist, shown side by side as two complementary styles, and between them a small handshake gesture hinting the two can be combined. Style friendly and approachable like a clever XKCD or Kurzgesagt panel, expressive faces and body language, one clear visual idea contrasting fill-in-the-blank reconstruction against match-the-pair sorting, minimal clutter, generous negative space, suitable for a technical textbook. No text, letters, numbers, or labels anywhere in the image.
alt: Two cartoon workers tackle the same wavy strip differently, one fills in small blanked gaps patch by patch for fine local detail while the other sorts whole strips into matching piles for the global gist, with a small handshake between them, contrasting masked reconstruction against contrastive learning and hinting they combine.
caption: Masked modeling rebuilds the fine local detail while contrastive learning sorts for the global gist; the two answer different questions, which is exactly why people increasingly use both.
